origin: A biblical genealogical statement in Ancient Greek, listing royal succession in Judah: “Solomon begat Rehoboam, Rehoboam begat Abijah, Abijah begat Asa.” The phrasing and structure strongly resemble a Septuagint/LXX-style rendering of Old Testament material (likely from the genealogical/regnal context of the kings of Judah, near passages like 1 Kings/2 Chronicles).
author: An anonymous ancient biblical author/scribe or (more likely) a translator/redactor responsible for the Greek text (e.g., a Septuagint translator or later copyist who preserved the LXX phrasing). The individual “author” is not a personal writer but a custodial religious text tradition.
thoughts: The authorial perspective is likely neutral and archival: the purpose is to preserve legitimate lineage and establish historical-theological continuity (who is the son of whom) rather than to argue or persuade. The motivation would be to document royal ancestry for readers who care about covenant history, fulfillment of prophecy, or dynastic legitimacy; any minor spelling variation (e.g., Ἀσάφ for what is expected as Asa) could reflect manuscript tradition or translation/orthographic differences rather than intent.
audience: Religious audiences familiar with biblical history—Jewish or early Christian readers/scribes studying scriptures in Greek, possibly in a liturgical, educational, or scholarly setting. The audience would be people who use genealogies to understand the sequence of kings and the broader narrative of Israel/Judah.
written_when: Most plausibly during the Hellenistic period of Greek scriptural translation (Septuagint era, ~3rd–2nd century BCE). Alternatively, if taken as a later Greek manuscript form, it could also reflect transmission/copying in the early centuries CE, but the linguistic register suggests an LXX-style Vorlage rather than a modern composition.
